title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text: Status: Design spec for the v1.1 overhaul. Builds directly on the Architecture forward-compat hooks (PlayCaller-as-interface, watch-vs-quick-sim as a presentation toggle, coach attributes feeding play-calling). The d20 check primitive in Mechanics is unchanged — v1.1 changes what gets composed into a play and how the result is presented, not the dice.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
1 What v1.1 is
v1 ships a believable, fully-simulated season you advance and read about. v1.1 makes a game something you watch and shape:

SimCast — a simulation broadcast of your team’s game. Not the 3D field (that’s later); a paced, legible, ESPN-style play-by-play you watch at a chosen speed, with the real rhythm of a football game (huddles, tempo changes, timeouts, TV breaks, the two-minute drill).
Gameplan — you set scheme, personnel, usage, reads, tempo, and aggression before kickoff; the AI play-caller honors it; you watch it play out. The knobs are real and they change the box score.
Playstyle identity — a real playbook of formations and concepts, offensive and defensive schemes, and coach attributes wired into play-calling so all ~130 teams play distinctly. Air Raid under an aggressive coordinator should look nothing like a smashmouth option team — on both sides of the ball.
Atmosphere — every program plays in a real stadium with a simulated crowd. Attendance is immersion (the broadcast shows the gate), a home-field edge in the sim (a loud house tilts the dice), and the first real driver of the future ticket → NIL economy.


The v1.1 north star: “Hit Play Game → SimCast, and watch your gameplan unfold in a real stadium against an opponent who has an identity of its own.”

This refines two v1 decisions (Decisions log): “sim & watch, no live play-calling” becomes sim & watch a gameplan you authored (still no live per-play input in SimCast), and “play catalog granularity” (open question #2) resolves toward a real playbook of formations + concepts.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
section: 2 Play Game — the game-day entry point
text:
2 Play Game — the game-day entry point
On your team’s game day, the matchup gets a Play Game dropdown with three options:







Option
Behavior




Playcaller (grayed out in v1.1)
The 3D gameday play-calling mode — you call every play from kickoff. The mode itself is later; the button and the IPlayCaller seam ship now. See Section 12.


SimCast
Watch the simulation broadcast — the v1.1 build (Section 3).


Quick Sim
Instant final + box score (today’s behavior; the timeline is still built so the game is reviewable play-by-play).



Non-user games are always quick-simmed (the whole week resolves instantly), exactly as v1 — SimCast/Playcaller are presentation choices over the same engine, so nothing about the sim changes based on how you watch.

Jump-In is not a Play Game option — it lives inside SimCast: while a broadcast is running you can jump into the ongoing match and take the play-calling over yourself (it hands off to Playcaller mid-game). Also grayed out in v1.1. See Section 12.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
3 SimCast — the broadcast
SimCast is pure presentation. The engine produces a deterministic game; SimCast plays it back. This keeps “same seed ⇒ same game” intact while the user scrubs speed.
It’s not one scrolling log — it’s a game-presentation hub with tabs, modeled on an ESPN gamecast. A persistent header carries the scoreboard, game clock, quarter, down & distance, ball-on, possession, timeouts, the speed control (0.5× / 1× / 2× / 4× / Instant), and a (disabled) Jump-In button (Section 12).







Tab
What it is




Gamecast
The live view: play-by-play streaming in real time as the clock runs, with light commentary (“3rd & 4 — they’re in hurry-up…”, “BLOCKED!”). The pauses are rendered, not skipped (Section 4). This is the thing you actually watch.


Play-by-Play
The structured history: each drive is an object — a collapsible card with its summary (Touchdown · 7 plays, 83 yds, 4:05) that expands to the individual plays. Mirrors the shared ESPN layout. Filter: All / Scoring / Key plays.


Box Score
Per-team passing / rushing / receiving / defense / returns / kicking / punting — the existing BoxScorePage, now updating live.


Team Stats
The team-comparison grid: 1st downs, 3rd/4th-down efficiency, total yards, yards/play, penalties, turnovers, time of possession.


Game Info
Stadium, location, attendance, weather, network (Section 8).



Speed only changes the playback cadence; it never touches the RNG or the outcome. Quick Sim lands you straight on the final Box Score, but the timeline is still built — so a quick-simmed game is reviewable play-by-play (the architecture’s spectator-mode hook).

3.1 The structured game timeline
The foundation for all of the above (and the thing the current engine lacks). Today the sim emits a List<string> play log; v1.1 emits a typed, drive-grouped timeline every tab reads from.
GameTimeline
└─ Drive[]                 ← a possession: team, start/end field pos, result, plays, yards, time of poss
   └─ Segment[]            ← ordered; each carries a clock stamp + a real-time "broadcast duration"
      ├─ Snap              ← a play: call (formation+concept), participants, the check chain, the result
      ├─ Pause             ← huddle / sub / timeout / TV timeout / 2-min / injury / measurement
      ├─ Score             ← TD / FG / XP / 2-pt / safety / return TD (the beats to emphasize)
      └─ PossessionChange  ← punt / turnover / downs / kickoff (with returns)

Drives are first-class — that’s what the Play-by-Play tab renders as cards, and what a drive chart summarizes. (Today drives are simulated but not retained as objects.)
The timeline is computed by the sim, deterministically. The string play log becomes a derived view of it, so logs, box scores, drive cards, and the Gamecast all read one source.
Broadcast duration per segment is a presentation hint (a sack lingers, a hurry-up snap is quick); the chosen speed multiplies it. It is not the football clock — that’s the game-clock runoff on each Snap/Pause (Section 4).

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
4 Clock & pacing fidelity
The “pauses in the record” require a real game-flow model. Today GameClock is an elapsed-seconds counter with fixed per-play runoff and no stoppages (deferred at M2). v1.1 makes it faithful to college rules — which differ from the NFL in two ways that matter:






College clock rules we model (current NCAA):

First down, in bounds → clock keeps running. Since the 2023 rule change, gaining a first down no longer stops the clock — except inside the last two minutes of a half, where a first down stops it. (This is the big NFL difference and it speeds college games up.)
Two-minute timeout (new in 2024). The clock stops at 2:00 in the 2nd and 4th quarters — a built-in stoppage, not a TV timeout.
Incomplete pass → clock stops until the next snap.
Out of bounds → clock stops, then restarts on the ready-for-play signal — except inside the last two minutes of a half, where it stays stopped until the snap.
Change of possession (punt / turnover / downs) and penalty → clock stops, restarts on the ready signal.
Score → clock stops for the try (XP/2-pt) and the kickoff.
Play clock 40s (25s after administrative stoppages); the pre-snap burn is where tempo lives.




On top of the stop rules:







Element
Model




Per-play runoff
Pre-snap burn (tempo) + the play’s duration. Huddle = normal; no-huddle/hurry-up = minimal pre-snap burn; bleed-clock = milk the play clock to ~:01.


Timeouts
3 per half per team; spent by game-management logic (stop the clock trailing late, ice a kicker, avoid delay-of-game), driven by coach GameManagement.


TV timeouts
Inserted at quarter breaks and after some changes of possession — the broadcast rhythm (separate from the 2-minute stop).


Two-minute drill
End-of-half behavior: hurry-up, get-out-of-bounds, spikes, clock awareness — gated by score + time + coach.


Tempo as identity
A team’s default tempo is part of its scheme/gameplan (Air Raid pushes pace; smashmouth bleeds it), so pacing itself differentiates teams.



Each stoppage becomes a Pause (or a runoff annotation on a Snap) in the timeline, so the Gamecast renders the rhythm and the football clock stays honest. Clock realism is what makes a comeback drive feel real — the difference between college and NFL clock math is the difference between a team having time and not.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
5 Gameplan — the deterministic control surface
The knobs. You set these pre-game; the AI caller honors them; they are part of the deterministic inputs (same gameplan + seed ⇒ same game). For non-user teams, an AI sets a gameplan from the team’s scheme, coach, and roster.







Knob
What it controls




Scheme
The team’s playbook + identity on each side of the ball (Section 6).


Personnel / on-field
Which players are in for a given personnel grouping (pulls from the depth chart per formation).


Carry share
How carries split across the RB room (feature back vs. committee).


Target share
How looks split across receivers (a true #1 vs. spread-it-around).


Read order
The 1st / 2nd / 3rd reads in the pass game (target priority within a concept).


QB archetype
Pocket passer (dropback, climbs the pocket) vs. dual-threat (designed QB runs, scramble, RPO pull) — changes the run/pass surface and the scramble branch.


Tempo
Default pace + situational (hurry-up when trailing, bleed when leading).


Aggression
4th-down go-for-it tendency, deep-shot rate, blitz rate (defense) — a baseline the coach modulates.



The depth chart (today just “starter ordering”) grows a thin usage layer: per-position role slots + the shares above. Promotion still works; usage is the new dimension.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
6 Schemes & the playbook
v1.1 commits to a real playbook of formations and concepts (open question #2, resolved). The d20 chain is unchanged; concepts decide which checks compose and with what modifiers.

6.1 The three tiers
Formation   ← personnel + alignment  (who's on the field, where)
   └─ Concept   ← the play             (what they run)
        └─ Check chain  ← from Mechanics — the d20 resolution, with concept-specific modifiers
Offensive formations (sample): Shotgun Spread (11), Singleback, I-Form, Pistol, Empty, Goal-line (heavy). Offensive concepts (sample):







Family
Concepts




Run
Inside Zone, Outside Zone, Power, Counter, Draw, QB Read/Option, Jet Sweep


Pass
Four Verts, Mesh, Smash, Flood, Slant-Flat, Screen (RB/bubble), Play-Action Shot


RPO
Zone-read + bubble / slant / glance (a single snap with a read that resolves run or quick throw)



Defensive fronts (sample): 4-3, 3-4, Nickel, Dime, 46/Bear, Goal-line. Defensive concepts (sample): Cover 1 (man-free), Cover 2, Cover 3, Cover 4 (quarters), Cover 0 blitz, zone blitz, stunt/base. A call = front + coverage + pressure.


6.2 A scheme is a weighted playbook
A Scheme is a distribution over formations + concepts plus identity modifiers:

Air Raid → heavy Shotgun/Empty; pass-weighted (Mesh, Verts, RPO); fast tempo; deep-shot lean.
Spread-RPO → Shotgun/Pistol; balanced run/pass via RPO; up-tempo.
Pro-Style → multiple personnel; balanced, play-action-heavy; pocket QB lean.
Smashmouth / Power → I-Form/Singleback; Power/Counter/PA; slow tempo.
Option → Pistol/Flexbone; zone-read/option/QB run; dual-threat QB.

Defensive schemes mirror this: 4-3 base, 3-4 multiple, nickel/dime pass-D, man-press aggressive, bend-don’t-break zone — each a distribution over fronts/coverages/pressures with a blitz-rate identity.


6.3 Read progression & QB archetype
A pass concept carries an ordered list of route options mapped to receiver roles (X / Z / slot / TE / RB) — the read order from the gameplan. On the snap:

The protection check sets a pressure clock (OL vs. rush).
The QB works the progression: read 1 vs. its defender → if covered, read 2 → read 3, each gated by QB ReadProgression + Awareness and shrinking pocket time.
Dual-threat QBs branch to scramble when the pocket breaks or reads are covered (and own the designed-run / RPO-pull concepts); pocket passers climb and check it down.

This is exactly the “1st / 2nd / 3rd reads, dual-threat vs pocket” surface — emergent from the concept × archetype × coverage matchup.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
7 Coaching styles
The Coach attribute sheet already exists (Architecture) and is unused by the sim today. v1.1 wires it in:







Attribute
Effect




PlayCalling (aggression/tendency)
Shifts the scheme distribution toward aggressive concepts; raises 4th-down go / deep-shot / blitz rates.


OffensiveAcumen / DefensiveAcumen
Grant advantage on the matchup when the call out-schemes the opponent; sharpen situational selection.


GameManagement
Drives clock/timeout decisions (Section 4) — when to burn a timeout, ice a kicker, go hurry-up.


Motivation
Composure/advantage buffs in big spots (already the architecture’s intent).



A team’s identity is the product: Scheme × Coach × Personnel × Gameplan. That product is what makes 130 teams distinct rather than 130 copies with different rosters.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
8 Stadiums & atmosphere
Every team gets a home stadium. v1.1 scope is location + capacity (the environment) — not the deferred economy plumbing.

Stadium — name, city, state, capacity (room later for surface, elevation, year built). It hangs off the program’s venue identity (and moves to the University / Athletics Department once the economy arrives — referenced by ID either way).
Data fill — assign each of the ~130 FBS programs its real home stadium (name / city / capacity), a one-time addition to FbsLeagueData alongside the existing team/conference/prestige data.

The SimCast Game Info panel surfaces stadium, city, attendance, and network — the ESPN “Game Information” card.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
9 Attendance simulation
Attendance isn’t flavor — it’s a sim input and the first real driver of the future economy.
What drives the gate (a deterministic function, like everything else in the sim):

Stadium capacity — the ceiling.
Home prestige + record — winners draw.
Opponent quality / ranking and rivalry — a ranked rival packs the house; a cupcake doesn’t.
Time of season + day/time slot — a November primetime conference game ≠ a Week 1 noon tune-up.
(Later: weather, TV window.)

→ yields an attendance number and a % of capacity — “how loud is it.”
Effect on the sim — home-field advantage. The engine has no HFA today; this adds it. Crowd strength (% of capacity) tilts the dice through the existing advantage system (Mechanics) — the architecture’s “home crowd grants advantage,” finally real:

The road offense feels it most: pre-snap communication / false-start risk and Composure checks on third down, in the red zone, and in the fourth quarter of a close game.
It scales with % of capacity (a half-empty stadium is no edge; a sold-out night game is) and is dampened by the road team’s Composure.
It is a deterministic input: same matchup + seed ⇒ same crowd ⇒ same edge.

The economic hook (seam now, flow later). Attendance → ticket revenue → Athletics Department budget → (v2) NIL / facilities / staff. v1.1 introduces attendance + HFA; the money flow stays in the v2 economy, but this is the input it consumes. Empty stadium → weak gate → no NIL → hard to stay competitive: the loop starts here.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
10 The IPlayCaller seam
Today PlayCaller is a static class with one passBias knob. v1.1 makes it the documented interface:





flowchart LR
  subgraph INPUTS
    SCH[Scheme<br/>weighted playbook]
    COA[Coach attrs]
    GP[Gameplan<br/>usage / reads / tempo / aggression]
    SIT[Situation<br/>down·dist·field·score·clock]
  end
  INPUTS --> PC{{IPlayCaller}}
  PC -->|picks formation + concept| PR[PlayResolver<br/>concept → check chain]
  PR -->|Snap + checks| TL[GameTimeline]
  TL --> SC[SimCast broadcast]
  TL --> BOX[Box score / stats]







AiPlayCaller composes Scheme × Coach × Gameplan × Situation → a concrete call. Both the user team (honoring the user’s gameplan) and every AI team use it.
UserPlayCaller is the seam left for Jump-In (Section 12) — a future implementation that takes calls from a human mid-game. Not built in v1.1; the interface and the disabled button are.
PlayResolver gains concept dispatch: each concept declares which checks it runs and its modifiers, composed from the same CheckResolver primitive.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
11 Determinism boundary
Unchanged in principle, explicit for the new pieces:

Gameplan + scheme + coach are deterministic inputs. Same save + seed + gameplans ⇒ identical game. The gameplan joins the seed as part of the reproducible state.
Attendance & home-field advantage are deterministic inputs too. The gate is a function of the matchup; the crowd edge it produces is computed before kickoff and feeds the dice the same way every replay. Crowd affects the sim; it is not presentation.
SimCast speed and all broadcast pacing are pure presentation. They read the already-computed timeline; they never call the RNG. Scrubbing speed, pausing, or replaying cannot change a single yard.
The timeline is an artifact, like media articles — derived from the deterministic game, never an input to it.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
12 Forward hook — Playcaller & Jump-In
The eventual 3D gameday play-calling mode (where you stop watching and call the plays) has two entry points, and v1.1 lays the seam for both so the later mode bolts on without a rewrite:

Playcaller — from the Play Game dropdown: enter the mode at kickoff and call every play. (Grayed out in v1.1.)
Jump-In — a control inside SimCast: while the broadcast runs, jump into the ongoing match and take the calls over from there. (Grayed out in v1.1.)

Both resolve to the same thing under the hood:

The IPlayCaller interface — a UserPlayCaller drops in for the user team in place of AiPlayCaller (Section 10).
The framing that SimCast and live-calling are two presentations over one engine — watching vs. driving — so “jump in” mid-game just swaps the user team’s caller, it doesn’t change the sim.

The 3D rendering and the live play-calling UX themselves remain out of scope (later than v1.1); v1.1 ships the interface and the two disabled buttons.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
13 Phasing
Each phase is independently demoable.

13.1 P1 — SimCast + pacing + atmosphere

The GameTimeline (drives → segments) + the string log as a derived view.
The college clock/pacing model: tempo runoff, the college stop rules, timeouts (coach-driven), TV timeouts, two-minute timeout.
Stadiums (data fill) + attendance + home-field advantage wired into the dice.
The SimCast hub: header (scoreboard/clock/speed/disabled Jump-In) + tabs Gamecast / Play-by-Play (drive cards) / Box Score / Team Stats / Game Info; the Play Game dropdown (Playcaller-disabled / SimCast / Quick Sim).
Demo: Play Game → SimCast, watch a real, paced game in a real stadium at 1× / 2× / instant, then drill the Play-by-Play drives.



13.2 P2 — Gameplan + usage knobs

The Gameplan model + the depth-chart usage layer (carry/target share, read order, personnel).
The IPlayCaller refactor (AiPlayCaller); resolver usage-weighting; QB archetype + read progression.
Gameplan editor UI for the user team.
Demo: author a gameplan (feature back, true #1 receiver, dual-threat QB) and watch the box score reflect it in SimCast.



13.3 P3 — Schemes & coaching styles

The playbook (formations + concepts) + concept dispatch in the resolver.
Offensive & defensive schemes as weighted playbooks; coach attributes wired into calling, clock, and advantage.
AI sets a per-team gameplan from scheme + coach + roster → league-wide distinct identities.
Demo: scout any team and see a real scheme; watch two contrasting styles (Air Raid vs. smashmouth) collide.



13.4 P4 — Polish & balance

Drive chart, SimCast narrative beats, richer stat overlays.
Balance pass so the new playstyles produce believable stat lines (scoring, run/pass splits, completion %, explosive-play rates) — the M9-style tuning, now with identities to tune against.
Demo: a multi-season run whose team-by-team stat profiles read like real, distinct programs.

title: v1.1 — SimCast & Playstyle Identity
text:
14 Open questions for v1.1

Playbook breadth — how many formations/concepts per scheme is enough for distinct identities without ballooning the caller/resolver? (Start lean, grow.)
Concept × coverage matchup table — the modifier matrix (e.g., Cover 0 vs. a deep shot = boom/bust; zone vs. mesh = soft) that makes scheme-vs-scheme legible.
Pacing realism vs. watchability — how much dead time to render before it drags, even at 1×; default speed and skip rules.
AI gameplan generation — how a team’s scheme + coach + roster yields a believable weekly gameplan (and how much it adapts week to week).
Timeline cost at scale — building the full timeline for ~65 quick-simmed games/week must stay near-instant (it’s cheap, but confirm).
Save growth — is a reviewable timeline persisted for the user’s games, or rebuilt on demand? (Likely rebuilt; only final/box persists.)
Jump-In handoff semantics — when the future live mode swaps the caller mid-game, how state/među determinism is framed (a documented seam now, resolved when that mode is built).
Home-field-advantage strength — how much edge a packed house should be; a tuning surface (too much and home teams never lose). Crowd grants advantage vs. a flat modifier — which, where?
Stadium data — sourcing the ~130 stadium names / cities / capacities, and handling realignment / relocations over many seasons.
Commentary depth — templated play descriptions vs. richer color for the Gamecast; can it reuse the Media INarrativeGenerator seam (templated now, LLM later)?

title: College Football Simulator
text:
A single-player college football season simulator built in Godot 4.6.3 with C# / .NET. You pick an FBS team, set your gameplan and depth chart, and simulate a full season — regular season, conference championships, and the complete CFP — to a national champion.

Outcomes aren’t formulas with noise bolted on. Every contested play resolves as a d20 check against a Difficulty Class, against players and coaches described by Baldur’s Gate III-style attribute sheets — no traditional “overall” ratings.

title: College Football Simulator
text:
Start here



🏛️ Architecture
How the world is modeled and how a game is simulated — the d20/DC engine, the attribute system, the institutional hierarchy, the class/data model, and the storage architecture.




🎲 Mechanics
The concrete numbers: rating scale, the skill/attribute blend, DC derivation, and the inside-run check chain. The build spec for Milestone 1.




🎯 v1 Goals
What v1 must do, what it deliberately won’t, the Definition of Done, and the milestone-by-milestone build order from engine to playable shell.




📌 Decisions Log
The locked, high-level decisions that anchor everything else — engine, language, simulation depth, dice system, and v1 scope.

title: College Football Simulator
text:
The shape of v1







Engine / language
Godot 4.6.3 · C# / .NET


Simulated
FBS (FBS-IAA) only — full current scheduling + the complete CFP


Game fidelity
Play-by-play (every down), with a quick-sim fast path


Resolution
d20 + attribute modifier vs. Difficulty Class (crits on 20, blunders on 1)


Ratings
Attribute & skill sheets (BG3-style) — no “overall”


v1 scope
Multiple seasons: pick a team → sim the season → crown a champion → offseason → repeat (recruiting placeholdered with generated walk-ons)


User control
Sim & watch (set scheme + depth chart, watch the sim resolve)


Media
In-game ESPN-style site: weekly recaps, Featured tab, team lookup — event-driven, templated in v1 (pluggable on-device LLM later)


Architecture
Event-driven core; save state split into per-module sidecars (current/season/historical/media) referenced by ID from a thin spine




These docs are living documents. They capture the plan we build against and evolve as the design firms up. See the Decisions Log for what’s locked vs. still open.

title: v1 Goals & Milestones
text: Companion to the Architecture. Living document.

title: v1 Goals & Milestones
text:
1 Vision
A single-player college football season simulator where the player picks an FBS team, sets their gameplan and depth chart, and simulates a full season — regular season, conference championships, and the complete CFP — to a national champion, then rolls forward through a lightweight offseason into the next season, year over year. Outcomes are driven by a Baldur’s Gate III-style character/attribute system resolved with d20-vs-DC dice. No traditional overall ratings.

The v1 north star: “Start a save, take a program, and run it season after season — believable, fully-simulated, year over year.”

title: v1 Goals & Milestones
text:
2 v1 Definition of Done
v1 ships when a user can, end-to-end:

Launch → Main Menu (New Save / Continue / Quit).
Start a new save: choose a conference, then a team within it; confirm season/year.
Land in the Season Hub: see schedule, roster, depth chart, standings, rankings.
Configure the team: set a gameplan/scheme and arrange the depth chart (sim-and-watch — no live play-calling).
Simulate games play-by-play, week by week (or sim-to-date), watching their team’s games resolve as a readable play log + live box score; other games resolve in the background.
Progress through the season: standings, rankings, and stats update each week.
Read the Media site: open the Media menu for weekly recaps, a Featured tab, and lookup of any team’s coverage (deterministic templated narratives in v1).
Reach the postseason: conference championship games → full CFP bracket → bowls → national champion crowned.
Roll into the next season: a lightweight offseason archives the year, advances class years, graduates seniors, and refills rosters with generated walk-ons — then a new season begins. Repeatable indefinitely.
Save & resume at any point with deterministic continuation.

If all of these work for any FBS team in any conference, across multiple seasons, v1 is done.

title: v1 Goals & Milestones
text:
3 Explicit Non-Goals for v1
(See the scope table for the full breakdown.) v1 does run multiple seasons, but the offseason replenishes rosters with generated walk-ons (player generation) rather than real recruiting. v1 does not include: real recruiting, transfer portal, coaching carousel, deep player progression, sub-FBS levels, live play-calling, 3D/2D field rendering, or any online/multiplayer features. These are deliberately deferred and the architecture leaves hooks for them.

title: v1 Goals & Milestones
text:
4 Milestones (build order)
Each milestone is independently demonstrable and builds on the last.

4.1 M0 — Foundations

Godot 4.6.3 C# project scaffolding, folder structure, test harness.
CheckResolver (the d20-vs-DC primitive) + seeded RNG + unit tests.
Demo: run thousands of checks, confirm distributions match expectations.



4.2 M1 — Data Model & a Single Play

Core Resource model: Player, Team, Coach with the starting attribute set.
PlayerGenerator (basic): build a position-appropriate roster from the seeded RNG.
PlayResolver for one play type (e.g. inside run) composed from CheckResolver.
Demo: generate two teams, simulate one play between them; inspect the check trace.



4.3 M2 — Full Game Engine

Drive/Game simulators: clock, downs, field position, scoring, turnovers, punts/FGs, basic penalties.
PlayCaller (AI offense + defense) driven by coach attributes + situation.
StatAggregator → box score.
EventBus + typed domain events (GameConcluded, etc.) emitted by the engine — the backbone consumers subscribe to.
Demo: simulate a complete, believable single game with a full box score and play log.



4.4 M3 — League, Schedule & Season

League populated with all FBS teams/conferences; PlayerGenerator scaled to fill all ~130 rosters (prestige-weighted); ScheduleBuilder.
SeasonSimulator (week loop), StandingsService, RankingService, tiebreaks.
Demo: sim an entire regular season; standings & rankings look sane.



4.5 M4 — Postseason

ConferenceChampionships → CfpBracketBuilder (current 12-team format) → bowls → champion.
Demo: sim regular season straight through to a crowned national champion.



4.6 M5 — Save / Load & Storage

SaveManager + ISaveModule: thin spine (registry, RNG state, manifest) + per-module sidecars (current / season / media), by-ID references, atomic writes, schema versioning.
Demo: save mid-season, quit, reload, continue deterministically; confirm sidecars load independently.



4.7 M6 — Offseason & Multi-Season

OffseasonStage: archive completed season → historical-state sidecar; advance class years; graduate/depart seniors; PlayerGenerator walk-on replenishment; build the next season.
Multi-season loop wired through SeasonSimulator; historical records accumulate.
Demo: finish a season, run the offseason, play a second season; confirm history persists and rosters are refilled.



4.8 M7 — UI & Flow (the playable shell)

Main menu, new-save flow (conference→team), Season Hub, depth-chart/gameplan screens, game viewer (play log + live box score), postseason bracket view.
Demo: the full Definition-of-Done experience (Section 2), start to finish.



4.9 M8 — Media & Narratives

MediaSubsystem subscribes to game/week events; builds a NarrativeContext per story; tiered coverage (featured/ranked/upset/user = full; rest = templated recap).
TemplateNarrativeGenerator (deterministic baseline) → NewsArticles written to the sidecar media cache on a background thread between weeks.
Media menu UI: weekly recaps, Featured tab, team lookup.
Pluggable hook left ready for LlmNarrativeGenerator (LLamaSharp / GGUF) as a later drop-in.
Demo: advance a week, open the Media site, read auto-generated recaps and the Featured page; look up any team.



4.10 M9 — Balance & Polish

Tune modifier/DC/margin curves against believable stat lines (scoring, yards, completion %, etc.).
Tune PlayerGenerator distributions so rosters (and walk-ons) look like real CFB talent spreads.
Demo: a multi-season run whose aggregate stats pass a “does this look like real CFB?” sniff test.


M0–M4 are engine; M5–M6 add persistence and the season-over-season loop; M7 makes it playable; M8 adds the media layer; M9 makes it feel right. M1–M6 can largely proceed headless with tests before the UI exists.

title: v1 Goals & Milestones
text:
5 Success Criteria (qualitative)

Believable: a simulated season’s leaders, scores, and upsets feel like real college football.
Legible: any outcome can be traced to the checks that produced it.
Deterministic: same save + seed reproduces the same season exactly.
Performant: simulating a full week (and a full season) is near-instant from the user’s perspective.
Extensible: adding FCS/D-II/JUCO later means adding data; real recruiting replaces walk-on replenishment in the existing offseason; new history/stat detail becomes a new sidecar — not an engine rewrite.

title: v1 Goals & Milestones
text:
6 Beyond v1 (roadmap sketch)

v1.1 — SimCast & playstyle identity: a simulation broadcast of your team’s game (tabbed gamecast, drive-by-drive play-by-play, speed-controlled, real college clock, no 3D); a pre-game gameplan the AI caller honors (personnel, usage, reads, QB archetype, tempo, aggression); a real playbook of formations + concepts with offensive & defensive schemes and coach attributes wired in, so all ~130 teams play distinctly; real stadiums + simulated attendance that drives home-field advantage (and seeds the future ticket → NIL economy). Lays the IPlayCaller + Playcaller / Jump-In seam for the later 3D live play-calling mode. See the v1.1 design.
v2 — Recruiting & development: replace walk-on replenishment with a real recruiting cycle; richer player progression/aging in the (already-present) offseason.
v3 — Movement: transfer portal, coaching carousel, contracts.
v4 — Expansion downward: FCS, D-II, D-III, NAIA, JUCO/community colleges as additional leagues/levels.
Media v1.x — on-device LLM writer: swap the templated generator for a local ~7B/GGUF (LLamaSharp) writer behind INarrativeGenerator — richer, more varied articles with no 3rd-party API.
Later — Interaction & presentation: live play-calling / coach mode, richer game visualization; more event consumers (awards, achievements, recruiting buzz).

title: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run
text: This page is the concrete spec for Milestone 1 (data model + PlayerGenerator + a single inside-run play). It pins the numbers the Architecture leaves abstract: the rating scale, the blend math, DC derivation, the inside-run check chain, and the first-pass curves. Curves here are deliberately first-pass and get tuned in M9 — the structure is what M1 commits to.
crumbs: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run

title: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run
text:
1 Ratings: scale & independent axes
Every rating — core attributes and position skills — lives on a 1–20 scale (BG3-pure). A Player carries two independent sets:

Core attributes (6): Strength, Agility, Speed, Awareness, Durability, Composure.
Position skills: the specific, checkable ratings for a position (for the inside run: OL RunBlock/AnchorStrength, DL BlockShed/RunStop, LB RunStop/Tackling, RB Vision/Elusiveness/Trucking/BallSecurity).


Why independent? Because checks blend a skill with an attribute (decision below), skills must not be derived from attributes — otherwise an attribute would count twice. The generator produces both axes with only a loose correlation (a fast RB tends to have higher Elusiveness, but they’re stored separately).

The 1–20 scale is intentionally coarse and dice-dominant. Granularity comes from the blend, not from a wide scale.
crumbs: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run

title: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run
text:
2 The check primitive
Every contested event is one d20 check (see the Resolution System for the philosophy):
roll   = d20
total  = roll + attackerModifier
DC     = 10 + defenderModifier          ← opposed check expressed as a static DC
success = roll == 20  OR  (roll != 1 AND total >= DC)
margin  = total - DC                     ← scales magnitude (e.g. yards)
crit    = roll == 20   (best-case, ignores DC)
blunder = roll == 1    (auto-fail, triggers a bad outcome)
Expressing the opposed check as DC = 10 + defenderMod keeps it legible and BG3-flavored: the defender is a passive difficulty, the attacker rolls against it.

2.1 The blend + modifier (the math)
A side’s modifier comes from blending the relevant skill with a supporting attribute, then applying the curve:
effectiveRating = w_skill · skill + w_attr · attribute       (both 1–20)
modifier        = floor((effectiveRating − 10) / 2)          (→ roughly −5..+5)

default weights : w_skill = 0.65 , w_attr = 0.35   (per-check tunable)
Blending the values before the curve (not the modifiers) is what produces many distinct effective ratings from a coarse 1–20 scale.


2.2 Advantage / disadvantage (the favorite lever)
When the two sides’ effective ratings are far apart, the stronger side rolls with advantage (2d20, take the higher); the weaker with disadvantage (take the lower):
if (attackerEffective − defenderEffective) >= ADV_GAP   → attacker rolls advantage
if (defenderEffective − attackerEffective) >= ADV_GAP   → attacker rolls disadvantage
ADV_GAP = 6   (first pass)
This is how a big talent gap makes a favorite reliably favored without widening the modifier range — the dice-dominant feel survives, but mismatches tilt correctly.
crumbs: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run

title: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run
text:
3 The inside-run recipe
One handoff up the middle resolves as a short chain of checks. Each link feeds the next; a decisive win or a crit can skip ahead.

3.1 1. The push (trenches)
The playside blocker vs the gap defender:




Attacker
Defender




Skill
OL RunBlock
DL BlockShed / RunStop


Attribute
Strength
Strength




Success → a hole opens; margin sizes it (and the yards at the line).
Blunder (nat 1) → TFL: defender wins decisively, negative yards, chain ends.
Crit (nat 20) → clean lane; skip straight toward open field.



3.2 2. The second level
If the back reaches the linebackers:









Attacker
Defender




Skill
RB Elusiveness (or Trucking vs power)
LB Tackling / RunStop


Attribute
Agility
Agility




Success → broken/avoided tackle, extra yards (YAC) from margin.
Failure → tackle made near the line of scrimmage.
Blunder or big hit at contact (ball-carrier nat 1, or a failure by a large margin) → trigger a fumble check: RB BallSecurity ⊕ Composure vs a fumble DC; failure = turnover.



3.3 3. Open field
Reached only via a decisive second-level win or a crit:




Attacker
Defender




Skill / Attr
RB Speed
pursuit Speed




margin scales the breakaway length — a true long gain or TD.
crumbs: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run

title: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run
text:
4 Margin → yards (first pass)
A single transfer curve turns a check margin into yardage; the chain sums the contributions.
yardsAtLine ≈ clamp( round(2 + margin · 0.8), −3, 12 )
secondLevel ≈ clamp( round(margin · 1.0),      0, 20 )   (on success)
openField   ≈ clamp( round(8 + margin · 2.5),  0, 80 )   (breakaway only)



Push margin
Result at the line




≤ −5 (or blunder)
TFL, −2 to −3


−4 … 0
stuffed, 0–2


1 … 4
gain, 3–5


5 … 8
good push, 6–10


9+ (or crit)
lane opens → second level




These constants are the M9 tuning surface. M1 commits to the shape (piecewise, margin-scaled, clamped), not the exact numbers.
crumbs: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run

title: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run
text:
5 Worked example
An inside run, traced end to end:
PUSH
  Playside Guard : RunBlock 14, Strength 15
                   eff = 0.65·14 + 0.35·15 = 14.35 → mod +2
  Interior DL    : BlockShed 12, Strength 13
                   eff = 0.65·12 + 0.35·13 = 12.35 → mod +1   → DC = 11
  gap = 2.0 (< 6) → no advantage
  roll d20 = 12 → total 14 vs DC 11 → SUCCESS, margin 3
  yardsAtLine = round(2 + 3·0.8) = 4   → hole opens

SECOND LEVEL
  RB  : Elusiveness 15, Agility 14 → eff 14.65 → mod +2
  LB  : Tackling 13,  Agility 12  → eff 12.65 → mod +1   → DC = 11
  roll d20 = 9 → total 11 vs DC 11 → SUCCESS, margin 0
  secondLevel = round(0·1.0) = 0   → tackle after a short gain

RESULT: +4 yards (no breakaway). A believable inside-run carry.
The point of the trace: every yard is explainable by the checks that produced it.
crumbs: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run

title: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run
text:
6 PlayerGenerator (basic)
Rosters are generated, deterministically, from the seeded RNG (same seed + team ⇒ same players):

Each attribute & skill ~ Normal(μ_position, σ ≈ 2–3), clamped 1–20. μ shifts with team prestige (a stronger program draws from higher means).
Axes stay independent: skills are only loosely correlated to their supporting attribute (enough to feel coherent, not derived).
Roster: generate enough to field the play — OL, interior DL, LBs, RB (a starters-plus set is fine for M1; full depth comes with M3).
Identity (stubbed): name from a pool, weighted class year, position-conventional jersey number.

Prestige-weighting and distribution shapes are first-pass; believable talent spreads are an M9 tuning target. The generator is also the seam recruiting replaces later — see player generation.
crumbs: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run

title: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run
text:
7 What this locks for M1

Ratings: 1–20, two independent axes (attributes + skills).
Check: d20 + attackerMod vs DC = 10 + defenderMod; crit/blunder on 20/1; margin scales magnitude.
Modifier: floor((0.65·skill + 0.35·attr − 10) / 2).
Big rating gaps → advantage/disadvantage (ADV_GAP = 6).
Inside run = push → second level → open field, with the margin→yards curves above.
Generator: seeded, per-position Normal, prestige-shifted, independent axes.

Everything numeric here is a tuning surface for M9; the structure is the M1 contract.
crumbs: Mechanics — Checks, Ratings & the Inside Run

title: Decisions Log
text: The high-level decisions that anchor the Architecture and v1 Goals. Each entry is something we’ve committed to (or explicitly deferred), so we don’t re-litigate it later.

Decision
Choice
Rationale




Engine
Godot 4.6.3
Target engine version; API checked against Godot 4.6 docs.


Language
C# / .NET (not GDScript)
Data model = custom Resource types; sim logic = plain testable C# classes.


Sim granularity
Play-by-play
Every down simulated; richest stats and most legible outcomes.


Resolution system
d20 + attribute modifier vs. Difficulty Class
BG3-style. Nat 20 = crit, nat 1 = blunder. Uniform mechanic across all events.


Ratings
Attribute & skill sheets — no “overall”
Results emerge from the math; “ratings” are just a derived lens.


v1 league scope
FBS (FBS-IAA) only
All conferences/teams at one tier; lower levels are additive data later.


v1 lifecycle scope
Multiple seasons (offseason = walk-on placeholder)
Sim a season + full CFP → champion → lightweight offseason → next season, indefinitely.


Player generation
Deterministic, prestige-weighted PlayerGenerator
Builds initial rosters and offseason walk-ons; the seam recruiting plugs into later.


Offseason (v1)
Archive → age roster → graduate → walk-on replenish
The v1 stand-in for recruiting; keeps rosters whole across seasons.


User control
Sim & watch
Set scheme + depth chart pre-game; no live play-calling in v1.


Game speed
Watch vs. quick-sim over one engine
Non-player fixtures default to quick-sim; quick-sim discards the play log.


Determinism
Single seeded RNG, saved with the game
Same save + seed ⇒ identical season; enables replay/debugging.


World hierarchy
Division → Conference → University → Athletics Dept → Team modeled in v1
Thin where unused, but present so the v2 economy bolts on without a rewrite.


Module communication
Event-driven — EventBus + typed domain events
Side effects via subscribers; the sim stays ignorant of its consumers.


Media subsystem
In-game ESPN-style Media menu in v1
Immersion; a downstream consumer of deterministic game data.


Narrative generation
Pluggable INarrativeGenerator; templated baseline in v1
LLM is a drop-in provider; de-risks v1 and keeps the Media menu working day one.


LLM runtime
LLamaSharp + bundled GGUF, on-device (~7B)
Fully self-contained — no 3rd-party API, no per-call cost.


Article coverage
Tiered (featured/ranked/upset/user = full; rest templated)
Quality-per-compute at ~65 games/week.


Article storage
Sidecar media cache per save
Persists & re-readable; keeps the core save lean.


Narrative determinism
LLM/narratives strictly downstream, never feed the sim
Preserves “same seed ⇒ same season.”


Storage model
Thin spine + per-module sidecars (current/season/historical/media/stats-index)
Keeps long multi-season runs lean; complexity grows by adding sidecars.


Cross-module references
By ID, resolved via the spine registry — never object pointers
Sidecars never point at each other or co-load; avoids coupling.


Cross-cutting data
A dedicated stats-index, updated incrementally from events
Leaderboards/records without loading every sidecar.

The v1.1 design commits to these (and refines two v1 calls above):








Decision

title: Architecture
text: Status: Living design document. Engine: Godot 4.6.3 · Language: C# / .NET · Genre: Single-player season-management simulation.

title: Architecture
text:
1 Design Philosophy
Three principles drive every decision below.

Simulation over presentation (for v1). The engine is the product. The UI exists to start a save, configure a team, and watch outcomes. We build a correct, observable simulation core first; polish comes later.
No “overall” rating — character sheets, not star ratings. Players, coaches, and staff are described by attributes and skills the way a Baldur’s Gate III character is. A player is not “an 87 OVR”; he is a bundle of numbers (Strength 14, Awareness 11, Route Running 16…) that produce emergent results. This makes development, scheme fit, and matchups fall out of the math rather than being hand-tuned.
Outcomes are dice, not formulas with noise bolted on. Every contested event resolves as a d20 check against a Difficulty Class (DC) modified by attributes. The randomness is the design, not a fudge factor. This keeps results legible (“he failed the block check, DL won the rep”) and tunable.

title: Architecture
section: 2 What We Simulate vs. What We Don’t
text:
2 What We Simulate vs. What We Don’t
This boundary is the most important thing in this document. Everything in “Out” is deliberately deferred so v1 can ship.

2.1 ✅ In scope (v1)







Area
Detail




League
FBS (a.k.a. FBS-IAA) only — all conferences, all teams.


Scheduling
The real current scheduling structure: conference + non-conference games, conference championship games.


Postseason
The full CFP slate (current format: 12-team bracket with byes, plus the bowl/championship structure feeding it).


Games
Play-by-play simulation — every down: down & distance, play call, d20 resolution, yardage, clock, scoring, turnovers, penalties, special teams.


Players
Full active rosters with BG3-style attributes/skills, position, year, depth-chart slot, fatigue, in-game/season injury state.


Coaches & Staff
Head coach + coordinators (+ position coaches as ratings) described by attributes that bias scheme, play-calling tendencies, and player development.


Stats
Per-play → per-game box scores → season aggregates (team & individual), standings, rankings.


User team
Pick one team in a conference; set scheme/gameplan and depth chart pre-game; sim & watch.


Game speed (watch vs. quick sim)
Two presentation modes over the same engine: watch (play-by-play log + live box score) and quick sim (instant final score + box score, play log discarded). The user may quick-sim any game, including their own. Non-player fixtures default to quick sim (a whole week’s other games resolve instantly).


World hierarchy
The full institutional pyramid (Division → Conference → University → Athletics Dept → Team) exists as data, even where v1 only puts behavior on the Team — see Section 3.


Media & narratives
An in-game ESPN-style site (Media menu): weekly recaps, season storylines, a Featured tab, and lookup for any team. Built on an event-driven pipeline; v1 ships deterministic, templated articles with a pluggable local-LLM writer that drops in behind the same interface — see Section 7.


Multiple seasons
v1 simulates season after season — finish a year, run a lightweight offseason, roll into the next, indefinitely.


Offseason (walk-on placeholder)
Between seasons: archive the year, advance class years, graduate/depart players, and fill open slots with generated walk-ons — the v1 stand-in for recruiting.


Player generation
A deterministic, prestige-weighted player generator populates initial rosters at new-save and the walk-ons each offseason — see Section 8.5.


Save/Load & modular storage
Persist and resume at any time. State is split into per-module sidecars (current / season / historical / media + a stats index) referenced by ID from a thin main-save spine — see Section 10.





2.2 ❌ Out of scope (v1 — deferred to later versions)







Area
Why deferred




Real recruiting
v1 runs multiple seasons but replenishes rosters with generated walk-ons (Section 8.5), not a recruiting cycle. Prospects, offers, visits, commitments are v2.


Transfer portal
Depends on player valuation & recruiting. Post-v1.


Coaching carousel
Hiring/firing/poaching, contracts. Post-v1.


Deep player progression
v1’s offseason advances class years and replaces departures with walk-ons; rich attribute development (coach-driven growth, redshirts, etc.) is v2.


Sub-FBS levels (FCS, D-II, D-III, NAIA, JUCO/community college)
Architecture must allow it (see Section 12), but v1 ships FBS only.


Live play-calling / coach-mode input during games
v1 is sim-and-watch. The engine is built so play-calling could be injected later, but the user does not call plays in v1.


3D/2D field visualization
v1 presents games as text/log + evolving box score. No animated field.


Spectator mode (watch non-player games play-by-play)
Non-player games are quick-simmed in v1. Watching another team’s game unfold play-by-play comes later — and because quick-sim already runs the full engine, it’s a presentation toggle (retain the play log), not an engine change.


Conference economy (TV deals → budgets → NIL/recruiting/facilities)
The world hierarchy that carries it exists in v1 (Section 3), but the money flow is v2+.


On-device LLM narrative writer
The Media pipeline + templated articles ship in v1; the local ~7B/GGUF writer (LLamaSharp) is pluggable behind INarrativeGenerator and lands once model size/perf/quality are validated — see Section 7.


Online / multiplayer / dynasty sharing
Not considered for v1.

title: Architecture
text:
3 World Hierarchy & Ownership Model
College football is not just teams and games — it’s an institutional pyramid where money and membership flow downward and results flow upward. Modeling this hierarchy correctly in v1 (even where most levels are thin) is what makes the v1→v2 leap possible: v2’s economy (TV deals → budgets → NIL, recruiting, facilities) bolts directly onto this skeleton instead of forcing a restructure.

3.1 The ownership pyramid
Division                     ── e.g. FBS (FBS-IAA). The competitive tier.
└─ Conference                ── membership group; negotiates media rights.
   ├─ TV / Media Deal        ── owned by the conference; distributes revenue to members.
   └─ University   (member)  ── the institution.
      └─ Athletics Department── holds the budget; runs all sports.
         └─ Team  (football) ── the football program — what we play & sim.
            ├─ Roster (Players)
            ├─ Coaches & Staff
            └─ Depth chart / scheme
Two distinct relationships live here, and keeping them separate is what makes realignment and economics clean later:

Containment / ownership (downward): Division → Conference → University → Athletics Department → Team → Roster. The literal tree of who-owns-what.
Membership (sideways): a University (via its Team) belongs to a Conference for competition and revenue-sharing. Conference realignment = re-pointing this membership edge without touching the ownership chain.

The TV/Media Deal hangs off the Conference, not the Team — deliberate and true to life: conferences sell media rights collectively and distribute to members through their athletics departments.


3.2 What each level holds — v1 vs. later








Level
v1 (modeled now)
Later (v2+)




Division (FBS-IAA)
Identity, its set of conferences, postseason/CFP rules.
Other divisions (FCS→JUCO); multi-division play.


Conference
Members, divisions/pods, scheduling rules, tiebreakers, championship-game config, auto-bid logic.
Realignment, expansion, TV deal negotiation.


TV / Media Deal
Stub attached to each conference (nominal value field, unused by sim).
Real contracts: value, term, distribution formula → feeds AD budgets.


University
Identity (name, location, brand, prestige).
Enrollment, academics affecting recruiting, brand growth.


Athletics Department
Thin: owns the football Team, carries a budget field (unused by v1 sim).
Budget from media/tickets/donors → allocates to NIL pool, recruiting spend, facility/stadium projects, coaching salaries.


Team (football)
Fully modeled: roster, staff, depth chart, scheme, record — the unit we simulate.
NIL spending, facility ratings affecting development, recruiting pitch.




Principle: v1 builds the whole tree but only puts behavior on Team (and the game engine). Division/Conference get the structural logic they need for scheduling & postseason; University/AthleticsDepartment/TVDeal exist as data shells with the fields v2 will animate. Nothing in v1 has to be torn down to add the economy.



3.3 The future economic flow (v2+, sketched now so v1 doesn’t block it)
Conference negotiates TV/Media Deal
        │  (revenue distribution)
        ▼
Athletics Department budget  ◀── + ticket sales, donors, NCAA/bowl payouts
        │  (allocation)
        ├─► NIL pool          ─► recruiting & transfer-portal competitiveness
        ├─► Recruiting spend   ─► prospect reach & evaluation
        ├─► Facilities/Stadium ─► player development, gameday advantage, revenue
        └─► Coaching salaries  ─► hiring/retaining better coach attribute sheets
This is not in v1, but every box maps to an entity in Section 3.1 — so v2 is populating fields and adding services, not restructuring the world.

title: Architecture
text:
4 The Resolution System (d20 + DC)
The beating heart of the engine. A single, reusable check resolves every contested football event.

4.1 The check
roll  = d20  (1–20)
total = roll + attributeModifier + situationalModifiers
result:
    roll == 20            → CRITICAL SUCCESS   (best-case outcome, ignores DC)
    roll == 1             → CRITICAL FAILURE   (blunder: fumble, sack, blown coverage)
    total >= DC           → SUCCESS
    total <  DC           → FAILURE
margin = total - DC       (used to scale magnitude, e.g. yards gained)

Attribute modifier: derived from the relevant attribute(s) (see Section 8). BG3-style: mod = floor((attr - 10) / 2) is our starting curve, but we keep this in one tunable function so we can reshape it.
DC: set by the opposing unit/player’s relevant attributes plus situation. A run block is OL.RunBlock checked against a DC derived from DL.BlockShed.
Advantage / Disadvantage: roll 2d20, take higher (advantage) or lower (disadvantage). Granted by scheme fit, coaching edges, momentum, home crowd, fatigue mismatches. This is how we express “edges” without inflating raw numbers.
Margin scales magnitude. Beating a DC by 1 ≠ beating it by 12. A run play’s yardage, a pass’s air yards, all scale off margin through a transfer curve.



4.2 Determinism & reproducibility
All randomness flows through a single seeded RandomNumberGenerator owned by the simulation context. Same save + same seed + same inputs ⇒ identical game. Essential for debugging, save integrity, and “replay this game” later. We never call GD.Randi() / global RNG inside sim logic.


4.3 Why this and not probability tables
A play-by-play d20 engine gives us legible, debuggable outcomes (“OL_LeftGuard failed RunBlock check vs DT, DC 16, rolled 7+4=11 → penetration, RB tackled for -2”) and a uniform mechanic across blocking, coverage, tackling, kicking, and decision-making. Tuning happens in modifier curves and DC tables, isolated from the dice.

title: Architecture
text:
5 Simulation Layers (top-down)
The sim is a nest of loops, each layer producing events the layer below resolves. Each layer is its own service so it can be tested in isolation.
SeasonSimulator
  └─ for each week:
       WeekSimulator
         └─ for each scheduled game:
              GameSimulator                      ← clock, score, possession, weather
                └─ DriveSimulator                ← field position, drive state
                     └─ PlaySimulator            ← chooses play, runs the play
                          ├─ PlayCaller          ← offense/defense pick plays (coach attrs + situation)
                          └─ PlayResolver        ← d20 checks → yards / TO / score / penalty
                               └─ CheckResolver  ← the d20-vs-DC primitive
  └─ after regular season: ConferenceChampionships → CFPBracket → bowls → Champion
  └─ after each week: update stats, standings, rankings, injuries, fatigue recovery
  └─ after the season: OffseasonStage
        ├─ archive completed season → historical state
        ├─ advance class years · graduate & depart seniors
        ├─ PlayerGenerator → fill open slots with walk-ons (v1 recruiting placeholder)
        └─ build next Season → loop   (v1 runs season after season)
Key idea: PlayResolver composes many CheckResolver calls. A single pass play might resolve: protection (OL vs DL checks) → QB decision (Awareness check) → throw (Accuracy vs DC from distance/coverage) → catch (Catching vs DC from coverage/contact) → YAC (Agility vs Tackling). Each is one d20 check; the play outcome is their composition.

title: Architecture
text:
6 Event-Driven Architecture & Side Effects
Game modules don’t reach into each other’s side effects. The simulation emits typed domain events, and independent subscribers react. This keeps the sim core ignorant of presentation, media, stats, and everything else — they’re all just listeners.

Producers: the simulators emit domain events as things happen — GameConcluded, WeekAdvanced, UpsetOccurred, RankingsUpdated, PlayerInjured, RecordBroken, SeasonConcluded.
Bus: EventBus (autoload) dispatches events to subscribers (typed C# events / Godot signals).
Consumers (side effects): the Media & Narrative subsystem (Section 7) is the flagship consumer; stats aggregation, standings/rankings refresh, injury bookkeeping, and — later — achievements, awards watches, and recruiting buzz all hang off the same events.

Two rules keep this clean:

Events are facts, not commands. A producer announces what happened; it never knows who listens. Adding a new consumer means subscribing, not editing the sim.
Consumers never mutate state the sim depends on. Side effects are strictly downstream. The deterministic core (sim + seeded RNG) is the single source of truth; consumers read events and produce their own artifacts (articles, stat tables, UI updates). This is exactly what lets non-deterministic work like LLM text generation live in the system without breaking “same seed ⇒ same season.”

Consumers may also process events asynchronously on worker threads (e.g. narrative generation between weeks), so advancing the season never blocks on a side effect.
SeasonSimulator / GameSimulator ──emit──▶ EventBus
                                            │  (GameConcluded, WeekAdvanced, UpsetOccurred …)
                ┌──────────────┬────────────┴───────────┬──────────────────┐
                ▼              ▼                         ▼                  ▼
         StatAggregator   RankingService          MediaSubsystem      (future: awards,
                                                  (async / threaded)   achievements, buzz)

title: Architecture
text:
7 Media & Narrative Subsystem
An in-game ESPN-style site for immersion: weekly recaps, season storylines, a Featured tab, and the ability to look up your team or any other team and read its coverage. It is pure presentation — a consumer of Section 6, derived from deterministic GameResult data.
Pipeline (between weeks, in the background):

WeekAdvanced / GameConcluded events land on the EventBus.
MediaSubsystem builds a NarrativeContext per story — the facts a writer needs: final score, key plays, standout stat lines, standings/ranking implications, streaks, rivalry/upset flags.
A tiering pass decides coverage and article type (game recap, upset alert, ranking shake-up, season storyline).
The chosen INarrativeGenerator turns each NarrativeContext into a NewsArticle.
Articles are written to the sidecar media cache and surfaced in the Media menu.

Steps 2–5 run off the main thread, kicked off when a week concludes, so the player can keep advancing; articles stream into the Media menu as they finish.
Coverage tiering (quality-per-compute; scales to ~65 games/week): featured / Top-25 / rivalry / upset / user-team games get full articles; everything else gets a short templated recap + box score. The Featured tab surfaces the week’s biggest stories.
Pluggable generation — INarrativeGenerator:

TemplateNarrativeGenerator — v1 baseline. Deterministic, instant, no model dependency; fills authored templates from the NarrativeContext. Always available, and the fallback for low-tier games.
LlmNarrativeGenerator — drop-in upgrade. A small local model (~7B) via LLamaSharp loading a bundled GGUF, fully on-device (no 3rd-party API, no per-call cost). It’s handed a constrained “script”: a structured prompt that injects the NarrativeContext facts and dictates format/length/tone, so a small model stays factual and on-brand. Slots in behind the same interface with zero changes elsewhere.

This mirrors the PlayCaller-as-interface pattern (Section 12): the consumer is stable; the implementation swaps.
Determinism boundary. Articles are artifacts, never inputs — regenerating one can’t change a score or a standing. Persisting them (below) also means a reloaded save shows the same text the player first read.
Storage — sidecar media cache. Articles persist in a per-save media store separate from the core SaveGame (e.g. user://saves/<slot>.media), keyed by week + team + article id. It survives reload, lets the user re-read past coverage, and keeps the core save lean.

title: Architecture
text:
8 Attribute & Skill Model (BG3-inspired)
No overall rating. A Player carries core attributes (broad, slow-changing, BG3’s six-stat analog) and position skills (specific, what actually gets checked on a play).

8.1 Core attributes (every player has these)
The working set (numbers, scale, and the blend math live in Mechanics):

Strength — power blocking, breaking/making tackles, press.
Agility — change of direction, route breaks, shedding.
Speed — straight-line; gates breakaways and pursuit.
Awareness — football IQ; reads, audibles, coverage recognition, penalty avoidance.
Durability — injury-resistance & fatigue curve (not a per-play check; modifies injury/fatigue rolls).
Composure — performance under pressure (late game, hostile crowd, big stage); affects advantage/disadvantage.



8.2 Position skills (subset relevant per position)
Examples (full matrix → attributes reference):

QB: ThrowPower, ShortAccuracy, DeepAccuracy, PlayAction, Scramble, ReadProgression.
RB: Vision, Elusiveness, Trucking, BallSecurity, PassBlock, Receiving.
WR/TE: RouteRunning, Catching, ContestedCatch, Release, RunBlock (TE).
OL: RunBlock, PassBlock, PullSpeed, AnchorStrength.
DL/EDGE: BlockShed, PassRush, RunStop, Pursuit.
LB: Coverage, RunStop, Blitz, Tackling.
DB: ManCoverage, ZoneCoverage, BallSkills, PressTechnique, Tackling.
ST: KickPower, KickAccuracy, PuntPower, Returns.

A check looks up the relevant skill (or a blend of skill + core attribute) for both participants and resolves through the system in Section 4.


8.3 Coaches & staff
Coaches are character sheets too — no “overall.” Attributes such as: OffensiveAcumen, DefensiveAcumen, PlayCalling (aggression/tendency), Development (per-position growth bias), Discipline (penalty rate), GameManagement (clock/timeouts/4th-down), Recruiting (stubbed for v1), Motivation (Composure/advantage buffs). Coach attributes feed the PlayCaller (what plays get chosen) and grant advantage/disadvantage at the check layer, rather than directly editing player numbers mid-game.


8.4 Derived vs. stored
We store attributes. We derive any “rating-like” number on demand (e.g. a scouting view) from attributes through a position-weighted function — never the reverse. This keeps the BG3 ethos intact and means a “rating” is just one lens on the sheet.


8.5 Player generation (initial rosters & walk-ons)
Rosters aren’t hand-authored — a deterministic PlayerGenerator builds them, drawing on the seeded RNG (Section 4) so a given save reproduces the same players. It serves two jobs:

Initial league fill at new-save: ~130 FBS teams’ worth of players with position-appropriate attribute/skill sheets, class years, names, and physicals.
Walk-on replenishment each offseason: the v1 stand-in for recruiting — open scholarship/depth slots are filled with generated walk-ons so rosters stay whole season to season.

Generation is prestige-weighted: higher-prestige programs draw from stronger distributions, a rough proxy for recruiting pull until real recruiting arrives. The generator is also the seam recruiting plugs into later — a RecruitingService replaces the walk-on step (job 2) without touching initial generation (job 1, which still seeds new leagues and, eventually, lower divisions).

title: Architecture
text:
9 Data Model — Core Classes
In Godot+C#, the persistent data model is built from custom Resource types ([GlobalClass] public partial class X : Resource). Resources serialize cleanly via ResourceSaver/ResourceLoader, show in the editor inspector, and are the idiomatic Godot way to hold game data. Pure-logic services (simulators) are plain C# classes, not nodes — they take data in and return results, keeping them unit-testable without the scene tree.
Resource (data — serialized)
├─ Player              : attributes, skills, position, class/year, depth slot, fatigue,
│                         injury status; current-season stats live in the season sidecar,
│                         career stat lines archive to the historical sidecar (by player ID)
├─ Coach               : role (HC/OC/DC), attributes, tendencies
├─ StaffMember         : trainers/specialists as attribute bundles (lightweight in v1)
├─ Team                : identity (name, abbr, colors, venue, prestige),
│                         roster (Player[]), staff, depth chart, scheme/gameplan, record
├─ AthleticsDepartment : owns the Team; budget field (thin in v1 — see World Hierarchy)
├─ University          : institution identity/brand; owns its AthleticsDepartment
├─ TVDeal              : media-rights stub attached to a Conference (thin in v1)
├─ Conference          : member universities/teams, divisions/pods, tiebreak rules,
│                         champ-game config, TVDeal
├─ Division            : competitive tier (FBS for v1); member conferences, postseason/CFP rules
├─ League              : root world container (holds the FBS Division in v1)
├─ Schedule            : weeks → list of Matchup (home, away, site, week, type)
├─ GameResult          : final score, box score, play log (empty when quick-simmed), key events
├─ Season              : year, current week, Schedule, standings, rankings, postseason state
└─ SaveGame (spine)    : entity registry (ID→sidecar) · user team id · current-season
                          pointer · RNG seed/state · schema version · sidecar manifest
                          (bulk data lives in sidecars it references by ID — see @sec-storage)

Plain C# (logic — not serialized)
├─ SeasonSimulator / WeekSimulator / GameSimulator / DriveSimulator / PlaySimulator
├─ PlayCaller (offense + defense)
├─ PlayResolver
├─ CheckResolver           ← the d20-vs-DC primitive
├─ RankingService, StandingsService, TiebreakService
├─ CfpBracketBuilder, ScheduleBuilder
├─ StatAggregator
├─ PlayerGenerator             ← deterministic roster & walk-on generation (@sec-player-gen)
├─ OffseasonStage              ← archive → age roster → walk-on replenish → next season
├─ SaveManager + ISaveModule   ← spine + per-module sidecar load/save (@sec-storage)
├─ EventBus + typed domain events (GameConcluded, WeekAdvanced, UpsetOccurred, …)
└─ MediaSubsystem + INarrativeGenerator (TemplateNarrativeGenerator | LlmNarrativeGenerator)

C# serialization note: Fields meant to persist must be Godot-visible — use [Export] properties and Godot collection types (Godot.Collections.Array<T>, Godot.Collections.Dictionary) on Resource subclasses, since plain List<T>/.NET dictionaries don’t serialize through ResourceSaver. Confirmed against Godot 4.6 docs.


Media types (see Section 7): NewsArticle : Resource (headline, body, type, week, team refs, timestamp) lives in the per-save sidecar media cache, not the core SaveGame graph. The EventBus, domain events, MediaSubsystem, and INarrativeGenerator (TemplateNarrativeGenerator / LlmNarrativeGenerator) are plain C#.

title: Architecture
text:
10 Storage & Save Architecture
Over a long, multi-season run the interesting data — detailed stats, player career logs, season history — only grows. To keep the hot path small and let that complexity expand gracefully, a save is not one monolithic file. It’s a thin spine plus a set of per-module sidecars, generalizing the media-cache pattern.

10.1 The spine (main save file)
Small, always loaded first. Holds only what’s needed to bootstrap and to resolve everything else:

Entity registry — ID → name → which sidecar for every entity. The one piece of glue that lets sidecars reference each other without knowing about each other’s files.
Pointers & identity — user team, current-season pointer, settings.
RNG seed + state — so a resumed save continues deterministically (Section 4).
Schema version + sidecar manifest — which files belong to this save, and at what version.

The original plan was ResourceSaver/ResourceLoader (.tres/.res).

As built (M5): to keep CfbSim.Core engine-free and headlessly testable, persistence is implemented as portable JSON (System.Text.Json) inside the core — one file per sidecar (spine.json, league.json, season.json, history.json, archive.json, media.json), written atomically (temp + rename) and version-stamped via the spine. The Godot side just hands the core a globalized user://saves/<slot> path. Same spine-plus-sidecars shape, just a different (Godot-independent) serializer. Box scores are an exception — kept in a per-session memory cache, not yet persisted.



10.2 Module sidecars (v1 partition)








Sidecar
Holds
Loaded when




current-state
The world as it is now: teams, rosters, players’ current attributes, staff, depth charts, schemes, prestige.
At load — it’s the live league.


season-state
The in-flight season: schedule, results, standings, rankings, week pointer, postseason bracket, current-season stats.
While playing a season.


historical-state
Completed seasons: final standings, champions, year-by-year team records, archived player career stat lines.
When browsing history / records.


media
NewsArticles, keyed by week + team (Section 7).
When the Media menu opens.


stats-index
Cross-cutting rollups: league leaders, all-time records.
For leaderboards; updated incrementally.




Don’t over-shard. This five-way split is the whole v1 partition — not a sidecar per entity. Granularity is a knob we turn later (see Growing complexity, below).



10.3 The one rule that makes it work: reference by ID, never by pointer
The trap isn’t “this data references other data” — almost everything does. The trap is two files that must be loaded together. We avoid it with a single discipline:

Cross-module links are stored as IDs (StringName/int), never as live Resource references.
IDs are resolved through the spine’s registry, which says which sidecar holds the target and loads it on demand. A sidecar references IDs, not other sidecars — they never point at each other directly, and none needs a sibling eagerly loaded to deserialize.


Godot specifics: if a Resource @exports another Resource, loading the parent eagerly pulls the child — exactly the coupling we don’t want across modules. So cross-module fields are plain ID values, resolved with ResourceLoader.Load on demand. Within a module, real sub-resource references are fine.



10.4 The cross-cutting exception
Some data is owned by one module but queried across all — national stat leaders, all-time records. Rebuilding those by loading every team’s sidecar would defeat the purpose, so they get a dedicated stats-index, maintained incrementally as domain events fire (Section 6). It’s the one deliberate central rollup.


10.5 Growing complexity over time
When a module’s dataset becomes genuinely heavy — say, full play-by-play career logs per player — it’s promoted to its own sidecar, referenced by ID like everything else. No other module changes; the registry just learns a new mapping. That’s the whole point: we can deepen stat-keeping and history without a migration that touches the rest of the game.


10.6 Integrity

Atomic writes: each sidecar is written to a temp file and renamed into place, so a crash mid-save can’t leave a half-written file.
Version-stamping: every sidecar carries the spine’s save/schema version; a mismatch on load is detectable (and migratable).
One abstraction, paid once: every module implements the same ISaveModule (Save / Load / version), so the machinery lives in one SaveManager and each module stays trivial — keeping this from becoming “significant complexity.”






flowchart TB
  subgraph SPINE["Main save · the spine (small, always loaded)"]
    REG["Entity registry<br/>ID → name → sidecar"]
    META["user team · current-season pointer<br/>RNG state · schema version · manifest"]
  end

  CUR["current-state sidecar<br/>teams · rosters · staff · depth charts"]
  SEA["season-state sidecar<br/>schedule · results · standings · bracket"]
  HIS["historical-state sidecar<br/>past seasons · champions · career stats"]
  MED["media sidecar<br/>NewsArticles"]
  IDX["stats-index sidecar<br/>league leaders · records"]

  SPINE ==>|loads on demand| CUR
  SPINE ==>|loads on demand| SEA
  SPINE ==>|loads on demand| HIS
  SPINE ==>|loads on demand| MED
  SPINE ==>|loads on demand| IDX

  CUR -.->|IDs resolved via registry| REG
  SEA -.->|IDs resolved via registry| REG
  HIS -.->|IDs resolved via registry| REG
  MED -.->|IDs resolved via registry| REG
  IDX -.->|IDs resolved via registry| REG






This refines the earlier single-SaveGame-graph idea: the SaveGame resource is now the spine, and the bulk lives in sidecars it references by ID. (We still avoid the node-group save()/JSON-line pattern from the Godot docs — that suits scene-tree-resident state; our model is data Resources, which ResourceSaver handles directly.)

title: Architecture
text:
11 Project Structure & Runtime Composition
CollegeFootballSimGoDot.sln
CollegeFootballSimGoDot.csproj   ← Godot game project (Godot.NET.Sdk); references the core
project.godot
/src
  /Game            ← Godot-side C# only: Nodes, UI, autoloads, Resource adapters
  /CfbSim.Core     ← engine-free .NET class library — the simulation:
     /Rng /Dice /Ratings /Checks /Model /Generation /Sim
     (later: /Services /Save)
/tests
  /CfbSim.Tests    ← xUnit, fully headless (dotnet test)
/samples
  /CfbSim.Demo     ← console runner for quick play traces
/docs              ← this Quarto design-doc site
/data              ← .tres data assets: teams, conferences, schedule templates

Core/engine split (refinement, in place since M1): the simulation lives in a CfbSim.Core class library with zero Godot dependency, so it’s unit-testable headlessly with dotnet test (no scene tree, no engine). The Godot project references it. Consequently the domain model is plain C# for now; Godot Resource types and serialization (Section 10) are introduced at the persistence boundary (M5), either by adding the Resource base to the model or via thin adapter Resources. Randomness uses our own seeded PCG32 (IRng) rather than Godot’s RNG, which keeps the core engine-free and gives stronger cross-platform reproducibility.

Autoload singletons (Godot autoloads):

GameManager — owns the active SaveGame, current screen flow, new-game/continue.
SimDriver — bridges UI requests (“sim this week”, “sim to end of season”) to the plain-C# simulators and surfaces results/events.
EventBus — the domain-event hub (Section 6). Simulators emit typed events; UI, stats, rankings, and the Media subsystem all subscribe — the sim never knows who listens.
MediaSubsystem — listens for game/week events and generates narratives on a background thread into the sidecar media cache, feeding the Media menu (Section 7).

The simulators themselves stay out of the scene tree — autoloads are thin coordinators that own data and call into pure logic.

title: Architecture
section: 12 Forward-Compatibility Hooks (so v2+ doesn’t require a rewrite)
text:
12 Forward-Compatibility Hooks (so v2+ doesn’t require a rewrite)
We don’t build these in v1, but the architecture must not preclude them:

The full world hierarchy already exists (Section 3) — Division/Conference/University/AthleticsDepartment/Team — so adding FCS→JUCO is new data, and the v2 economy (TV deals → budgets → NIL/recruiting/facilities) is new services on existing entities, not a restructure.
PlayCaller is an interface — a UserPlayCaller can replace the AI for the user’s team later (live play-calling) without touching PlayResolver.
The same play-by-play engine backs both watch and quick-sim (quick-sim just discards the play log), so spectator mode for non-player games is later a UI toggle to retain that log, not a new engine.
The event-driven core (Section 6) is open for new consumers — achievements, awards watches, social-media buzz, recruiting interest — which subscribe to existing events rather than touching the sim.
INarrativeGenerator lets the on-device LLM writer replace the templated baseline (Section 7) with no changes anywhere else.
The offseason stage is real in v1 (walk-on replenishment) — so recruiting, the transfer portal, and the coaching carousel plug into an existing seam, not a new one. A RecruitingService later replaces PlayerGenerator’s walk-on step behind the same call.
Player already carries class/year + career history, and the historical sidecar already archives it, so richer progression/aging is added depth, not new plumbing.
New per-module sidecars can be added without touching existing ones (Section 10) — by-ID references resolved through the registry mean a future heavy dataset (e.g. detailed player career logs) just becomes its own sidecar.
Everything resolves through CheckResolver, so balance changes are localized to modifier/DC curves.

title: Architecture
text:
13 Open Questions (to resolve before/while building)

Exact attribute & skill list — needs its own attributes reference and the position→skill matrix + the attr → modifier and margin → yards curves.
Play catalog — how granular is the playbook? (formations + concepts vs. abstract “run inside / play-action deep” buckets). Affects PlayCaller complexity.
Clock & game-flow fidelity — real play-clock/game-clock model, quarter length scaling, hurry-up/2-minute logic.
Penalties & special teams depth for v1.
Data sourcing & generation — where real FBS team/conference identity data comes from, and the PlayerGenerator distributions (per-position, prestige-weighted) that yield believable initial rosters and walk-ons.
CFP format specifics — pin the exact current bracket/seeding/bye rules and conference-champ auto-bids.
Quick-sim performance — confirm full-engine quick-sim of a whole week (~65 games) is fast enough, or whether an abstracted fast path is needed.
Local-LLM specifics — which ~7B model, quantization/size vs. quality, GGUF licensing for redistribution, and the per-week background-generation time budget.
Narrative “script” design — the structured prompt/template per article type, and hallucination guardrails that constrain the model to NarrativeContext facts.
Offseason depth & save growth — does v1’s offseason apply any development/aging or strictly advance years + walk-on replenish? And what’s the expected sidecar (esp. historical) growth over many seasons?

These are tracked as the next design passes; none block agreeing on the architecture above.
